items.q: What property do shape memory alloys have?
items.correct: They return to a pre-set shape when heated.
items.misconception: They remember every shape they have ever been bent into.
items.why: Students think SMAs are infinitely programmable. They actually have ONE 'remembered' shape, set during high-temperature treatment. Heating restores that shape; the alloy doesn't store a history of deformations.

items.q: What does an LDR's resistance do as light intensity INCREASES?
items.why: Students assume 'more input = more resistance', but LDR resistance is INVERSELY proportional to light. Dark = high resistance; bright = low resistance. This is why LDRs detect darkness in circuits.

items.q: If voltage is doubled and resistance is unchanged, what happens to the current?
items.correct: Current doubles (V = IR, so I = V/R).
items.why: Students get the direction of proportionality wrong. Current is PROPORTIONAL to voltage (when R is constant) - more push, more flow. They sometimes confuse this with current being inversely proportional to resistance.
The answer examiners want is: Specific group product is for. “Anyone” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: Made-up realistic user. “Real customer” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In this lesson, how many user-design concepts are listed (6 total)?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
